content.text: A civilisation represents an advanced stage of human society. It is not merely a collection of settlements, but a complex social, economic, and cultural organisation with distinct characteristics that set it apart from simpler societies.

content.title: Civilisation
content.text: An advanced stage of human society characterised by organised government, urban centres, diverse economic activities, writing systems, and shared cultural values.

content.title: Essential Characteristics of a Civilisation
items: Government and Administration: Organised systems to manage complex societies and their activities.
Urbanism: Town-planning with cities, water management systems, drainage, and sewage facilities.
Crafts and Specialisation: Variety of crafts involving raw materials (stone, metal) and production of finished goods (ornaments, tools).
Trade: Both internal trade (within a city or region) and external trade (with distant regions and other parts of the world).
Writing System: A system of writing for record-keeping and communication.
Cultural Expression: Shared ideas and values expressed through art, architecture, literature, and social customs.
Agriculture: Productive farming to feed both rural villages and urban populations.

content.text: Each of these characteristics is crucial for the functioning of a civilisation. Among them, productive agriculture stands out as fundamental, as it must generate enough surplus to feed not only farmers but also urban populations engaged in other occupations.

content.title: Metallurgy
content.text: The techniques of extracting metals from nature, purifying or combining them, and studying their properties. This includes the knowledge of how to work with metals to create tools and ornaments.

Civilisations did not emerge everywhere at the same time. Different regions of the world developed civilisations in different periods. The earliest known civilisations arose in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq and Syria) around 6000 years ago, followed by ancient Egypt a few centuries later. The Indus-Sarasvatī civilisation, one of the oldest in the world, developed around 4500 years ago (from about 2600 to 1900 BCE).

content.title: Key Point
content.text: The emergence of civilisation depends on geographical factors such as river systems, climate, and soil fertility, which made agriculture possible and attracted human settlements.

notes.section_title: From Village to City: The Indus-Sarasvatī Civilisation

content.text: The vast plains of the Punjab and Sindh (now in Pakistan) watered by the Indus River and its tributaries became the cradle of one of the world's earliest civilisations. These fertile plains, enriched by river deposits and favourable climate, supported intensive agriculture. A little further east, the Sarasvatī River flowed from the foothills of the Himalayas through Haryana, Punjab, Rajasthan, and Gujarat.

Around 3500 BCE, villages in this entire region began to grow. With increasing trade and other exchanges, these towns further developed into cities. This transition from rural to urban centres happened around 2600 BCE, marking the emergence of a full-fledged civilisation.

content.title: Tributary
content.text: A river that flows into a larger river or lake. For example, the Yamuna is a tributary of the Ganga.

This civilisation is known by several names — 'Indus', 'Harappan', 'Indus-Sarasvatī', or 'Sindhu-Sarasvatī' civilisation. The name 'Harappan' comes from the city of Harappa (in modern Punjab, Pakistan), which was the first site of this civilisation to be excavated in 1920-21. The inhabitants of this civilisation are called 'Harappans'.

content.title: Why Multiple Names?
content.text: The term 'Indus Valley Civilisation' is now considered incomplete because archaeological evidence shows the civilisation extended far beyond the Indus region. It also flourished along the Sarasvatī River basin and its tributaries, making 'Indus-Sarasvatī Civilisation' a more accurate name.

The discovery of this civilisation spans over a century, beginning with the excavation of Harappa. Subsequent excavations at Mohenjo-daro (1922), Kalibangan, Dholavira, Rakhigarhi, and hundreds of smaller sites have revealed the vast extent and sophistication of this civilisation.

The Sarasvatī River, once a major river in the region, is now known as 'Ghaggar' in India and 'Hakra' in Pakistan. This river is now seasonal, flowing only during the rainy season. The Sarasvatī is first mentioned in ancient texts called the Ṛig Veda, where it is described as flowing 'from the mountain to the sea'. Later texts describe the river as drying up and eventually disappearing, which likely occurred around 1900 BCE.

The Harappan cities reveal a high level of urban planning and organisation. Major cities like Harappa and Mohenjo-daro (both in modern Pakistan), along with Dholavira (Gujarat), Kalibangan (Rajasthan), and Rakhigarhi (Haryana), were laid out according to precise plans with systematic arrangements.

content.title: Features of Harappan Urban Planning
items: Wide streets oriented to the cardinal directions (north, south, east, west).
Fortification walls surrounding the city for protection.
Distinct zones: 'upper town' (citadel) where the elite lived, and 'lower town' where common people lived.
Individual houses of various sizes lining the streets and smaller lanes.
Large public buildings used for collective purposes such as storage and administration.
Dholavira had three distinct zones instead of the usual two zones found in other cities.

content.title: Fortification
content.text: Massive walls surrounding a settlement or city, generally built for protective purposes.

content.title: Elite
content.text: The higher layers of society, including rulers, officials, administrators, and priests.

All buildings, whether large or small, were constructed using baked bricks of standardised sizes. This uniformity in construction materials and techniques across different sites suggests a centralised system of control and planning.

One of the most famous structures in Harappan civilisation is the 'Great Bath' at Mohenjo-daro. This small but elaborate tank measured about 12 × 7 metres and had sophisticated waterproofing made from natural bitumen (a tar-like substance) applied over carefully laid bricks. The tank was surrounded by small rooms, one of which contained a well. A drainage system allowed the tank to be emptied and refilled with fresh water.

The exact purpose of the Great Bath remains debated among archaeologists. Proposed interpretations include: (1) a public bathing facility, (2) a royal bath reserved for the elite, or (3) a tank used for religious rituals. The first interpretation has been largely ruled out because excavations show that individual houses in the city had their own bathrooms.

content.title: Architectural Sophistication
content.text: The Great Bath demonstrates the Harappans' advanced understanding of water management, engineering, and construction. The use of natural bitumen for waterproofing shows their knowledge of materials and their properties.

The Harappans gave much importance to water management and cleanliness. Houses often had separate areas for bathing, and these were connected to a network of drains that ran below streets to dispose of waste water. This sophisticated drainage system demonstrates their concern for sanitation and public health.

In Mohenjo-daro, evidence shows that people drew water from hundreds of brick-made wells. The presence of multiple wells distributed throughout the city indicates well-planned water supply systems. In other regions with different geographical conditions, people may have relied on ponds, nearby streams, or human-made reservoirs.

topics.topic_name: Reservoirs and Water Harvesting at Dholavira

content.text: Dholavira, located in the Rann of Kutch (Gujarat), shows the most elaborate water management system. The largest reservoir in Dholavira measured 73 metres in length — nearly the size of two football grounds! This demonstrates the Harappans' engineering capability and their ability to construct massive structures.

At least six large reservoirs were built at Dholavira, constructed with stones or cut directly into the rock. Most of them were connected through underground drains for efficient water harvesting and distribution. Such sophisticated systems would have required significant labour, planning, and administrative oversight.

content.title: Reservoir
content.text: A large natural or artificial place where water is stored for future use.

content.title: Implications of Water Management Systems
content.text: The scale and sophistication of water management systems indicate a highly organised administration. The construction and maintenance of such systems required coordination of labour, resources, and knowledge, suggesting the existence of a centralised authority and civic planning.

The Harappans created many settlements along the banks of large and small rivers. This choice was logical not only for easy access to water but also because rivers enriched the soil through flooding, making agriculture productive.

Archaeological findings show that the Harappans grew cereals like barley and wheat, sometimes rice, along with pulses (beans, peas, lentils) and a variety of vegetables. They were the first in Eurasia to grow cotton, which they used to weave into clothes, making them pioneers in cotton textile production.

content.title: Agricultural Products of the Harappans
items: Cereals: Barley, wheat, and sometimes rice.
Pulses: Beans, peas, and lentils.
Vegetables: Various types of vegetables.
Cotton: First in Eurasia to cultivate for textile production.
Tools: They made farming tools including ploughs, some designs of which are still used by modern farmers.

content.title: Pulses
content.text: A category of crops that includes beans, peas, and lentils (dal), providing essential proteins.

The intense agricultural activity was essential for supporting hundreds of small rural sites and large urban centres. Cities could survive only if sufficient agricultural produce from rural areas reached them regularly.

The Harappans also domesticated animals for meat consumption and fished in rivers and the sea. The large number of animal and fish bones found during excavations provides evidence of this practice. Scientific examinations of cooking pots have revealed remains of dairy products, turmeric, ginger, and banana, showing that the Harappan diet was diverse and well-rounded.

content.title: Dietary Diversity
content.text: The Harappan diet included cereals, pulses, vegetables, meat, fish, dairy products, and spices, indicating a balanced and nutritious food system that supported a large population.

The Harappans were engaged in active trade, not only within their own civilisation (between nearby or distant cities) but also with other civilisations and cultures within and outside the Indian subcontinent. This widespread trade network indicates a sophisticated economy and strong commercial relationships.

They exported ornaments, timber, objects of daily use, and possibly gold and cotton to distant regions. In return, they imported goods that were not available locally, such as copper and other materials. The most favoured ornaments were beads of carnelian, a reddish semiprecious stone found mostly in Gujarat.

content.title: Carnelian
content.text: A reddish semiprecious stone found mostly in Gujarat, highly valued by Harappans for making ornaments and beads.

The Harappans developed special techniques to drill carnelian beads, allowing strings to pass through them, and decorated them in various ways. They also worked conch shells into beautiful shell bangles, which required sophisticated techniques because shell is a hard material. Such craftsmanship indicates high skill levels and specialisation in production.

topics.topic_name: Maritime Trade and the Dockyard at Lothal

content.text: To conduct such extensive trade, the Harappans used land routes, rivers, and the sea for more distant destinations. This marked the first intensive maritime activity in India. Many Harappan settlements are located in coastal regions of Gujarat and Sindh, supporting evidence of maritime commerce.

Lothal, a small settlement in Gujarat, contains a remarkable structure — a dockyard, which is a structure used to receive and send boats for the transportation of goods. The dockyard has a basin measuring 217 metres in length and 36 metres in width, approximately the size of two football grounds. This massive construction demonstrates the importance of sea trade to the Harappan economy.

content.title: First Maritime Infrastructure
content.text: The dockyard at Lothal represents the first known maritime infrastructure in India, showing the Harappans' commitment to organised sea trade and their engineering capabilities.

Such elaborate trade networks require traders to be able to identify and verify goods — and also recognise each other! This seems to have been the chief purpose of thousands of small seals excavated from various Harappan settlements.

These seals were generally made of steatite, a soft stone that could be hardened through heating. They measure only a few centimetres and generally depict animal figures with a few signs above them. These signs are part of a writing system, though the exact meaning remains unknown. What is certain is that the animal figures and signs relate to trade activities.

content.title: Steatite
content.text: A soft stone that can be hardened through heating, used by the Harappans to make seals.

content.title: Harappan Script
content.text: The Harappan writing system remains undeciphered despite extensive research. The script appears on seals, pottery, and other objects, but scholars have not yet definitively decoded its meaning. This remains one of the major puzzles in understanding Harappan civilisation.

notes.section_title: Objects of Daily Life and Cultural Expression

content.text: Archaeological excavations have uncovered numerous objects made and used by Harappans in their daily lives. These artefacts provide insights into their lifestyle, activities, and values.

content.title: Objects of Daily Use Found in Harappan Sites
items: Bronze mirrors and copper objects for personal use.
Terracotta pots and vessels for cooking and storage.
Stone weights for trade and commercial transactions.
Bronze chisels and metal tools for crafts.
Gaming boards engraved on stone for entertainment.
Terracotta whistles and toys for children and adults.

content.text: These objects show that the Harappans had a well-developed material culture with a clear distinction between luxury items and utilitarian objects. The variety of objects also suggests specialisation in craft production and a complex consumer economy.
Beyond objects of daily use, Harappan archaeological sites have yielded remarkable artistic pieces that reveal their cultural values and spiritual beliefs.

content.title: Cultural and Symbolic Objects
items: Figurines and statuettes depicting human forms, including a famous 'Priest King' figure.
Seals with swastika symbols and depictions of deities.
A seal depicting a three-faced deity surrounded by powerful animals, suggesting religious or spiritual significance.

The 'Dancing Girl' — a bronze figurine from Mohenjo-daro about 10.8 cm high, notable for its dynamic posture and detailed bangles, suggesting the importance of dance and adornment in Harappan culture.
Terracotta figurines in various poses, including seated figures.
Designs on pots depicting scenes, such as a design at Lothal illustrating the story of a thirsty crow drinking water, which is a well-known fable that survived for over 4,000 years.

content.text: The 'Dancing Girl' figurine is particularly significant as it shows the artist's attention to detail and movement. The figure's multiple bangles worn on both arms — a practice still visible in parts of Gujarat and Rajasthan — suggests continuity of cultural traditions from ancient times to the present day.

content.title: Cultural Continuity
content.text: Many elements of Harappan culture appear to have survived and influenced later Indian civilisations, suggesting cultural continuity rather than complete discontinuity during the transition from Harappan to later periods.

notes.section_title: The Decline of the Indus-Sarasvatī Civilisation

topics.topic_name: The End of the Harappan Civilisation

content.text: Around 1900 BCE, the Indus-Sarasvatī civilisation, despite all its achievements, began to decline and eventually collapsed. The cities were abandoned one by one. If any inhabitants remained, they adopted a rural lifestyle in the changed environment. It appears that the earlier government or administration no longer existed. Gradually, the Harappans scattered over hundreds or even thousands of small rural settlements.

People eventually returned to a rural way of life, possibly because rural settlements provided easier access to food and water compared to cities. Cities, by contrast, depended on villages to provide food, water, and other resources.

For many years, scholars thought that warfare or invasions destroyed the Harappan cities. However, there is no archaeological evidence of warfare or destruction. The Harappans do not seem to have kept armies or weapons, and as far as available evidence indicates, it was a relatively peaceful civilisation.

content.title: Proposed Causes of the Harappan Decline
items: Climatic Change: A major climatic change affected much of the world from 2200 BCE onward, causing reduced rainfall and a drier phase. This would have made agriculture more difficult and reduced food supply to cities.
Drying of the Sarasvatī River: The Sarasvatī River dried up in its central basin, possibly suddenly. Cities that depended on the river, such as Kalibangan and Banawali, were abandoned.
Combined Environmental Stress: The combination of climatic change and water scarcity likely made it impossible for urban centres to sustain large populations.

content.text: These factors remind us of how much we depend on climate and the environment for our well-being. Environmental degradation can have profound consequences for human societies, even ones as advanced and organised as the Harappan civilisation.

Although the cities disappeared around 1900 BCE, much of the Harappan culture and technology was not entirely lost. Elements of Harappan culture, knowledge, and practices were passed on to the next phase of Indian civilisation, showing cultural continuity rather than complete break.

The study of the Harappan civilisation has revealed that earlier societies were just as sophisticated, organised, and capable of monumental achievements as many later civilisations. The Harappans created one of the world's earliest urban civilisations with remarkable achievements in town-planning, water management, trade, and crafts.

glossary.definition: An advanced stage of human society characterised by organised government, urban centres, diverse economic activities, a writing system, and shared cultural values.
The inhabitants of the Indus-Sarasvatī civilisation, named after the city of Harappa where the first excavations took place.
An ancient civilisation that flourished in the Indian subcontinent from approximately 2600 to 1900 BCE, one of the world's oldest and most extensive early civilisations.
Massive walls surrounding a settlement or city, generally built for protective purposes.
The higher layers of society, including rulers, officials, administrators, and priests.
A river that flows into a larger river or lake.
A category of crops that includes beans, peas, and lentils, providing essential proteins.
The techniques of extracting metals from nature, purifying or combining them, and studying their properties.
A reddish semiprecious stone found mostly in Gujarat, highly valued by Harappans for making ornaments and beads.
A soft stone that can be hardened through heating, used by the Harappans to make seals.
A structure used to receive, send, and maintain boats for maritime trade and transportation of goods.
A large natural or artificial place where water is stored for future use.
The process of growth and development of cities and towns, marking the transition from rural to urban settlement patterns.

summary_points: A civilisation is an advanced stage of human society characterised by organised government, urban centres, diverse crafts, trade, writing systems, and cultural expression, supported by productive agriculture.

The Indus-Sarasvatī civilisation (also called Harappan civilisation) flourished from approximately 2600 to 1900 BCE in the fertile plains of the Indus and Sarasvatī river basins, making it one of the world's oldest and most extensive early civilisations.

Harappan cities were carefully planned with wide streets oriented to cardinal directions, fortification walls, distinct zones for elite and common people, and sophisticated drainage systems, demonstrating advanced urban planning.
The Harappans excelled in water management, constructing wells, drainage systems, and massive reservoirs like those at Dholavira, showing their engineering knowledge and administrative capabilities.

The Harappan economy was based on agriculture (barley, wheat, rice, pulses, cotton), diverse crafts (carnelian beads, shell bangles), and extensive trade networks across land, rivers, and seas, with a dockyard at Lothal serving maritime commerce.

Harappan seals, made of steatite and bearing undeciphered script and animal figures, are believed to have been used for trade identification and record-keeping, though their exact meaning remains unknown.

Harappan culture produced diverse objects of daily use (mirrors, pots, weights, tools, games) and remarkable artistic pieces (the 'Dancing Girl' figurine, statuettes, symbolic seals), reflecting a sophisticated material and cultural life.

The civilisation declined around 1900 BCE due to climatic changes causing reduced rainfall and the drying up of the Sarasvatī River, making agriculture difficult and forcing people to return to rural settlements.
Although Harappan cities disappeared, much of their culture, knowledge, and practices were transmitted to later phases of Indian civilisation, indicating cultural continuity and a lasting legacy.
